questions.subcategory: Safety and Infection Control / Medication Safety
questions.stem: A nurse is about to administer a scheduled dose of digoxin 0.25 mg IV to a patient with heart failure. The patient's apical heart rate is 54 bpm. What should the nurse do FIRST?

options.text: Administer the dose as scheduled since the order is valid
Hold the dose and notify the provider of the patient's heart rate
Administer half the dose and recheck the heart rate in 30 minutes
Check the patient's most recent serum digoxin level before acting

questions.rationale: The standard safety parameter for digoxin is to hold the dose and notify the provider if the apical pulse is < 60 bpm in adults. Digoxin slows AV conduction; administering it to a patient with a heart rate of 54 bpm risks further bradycardia, heart block, and cardiovascular compromise. Administering the full dose ignores a critical safety parameter. Administering half the dose is not within standard nursing scope without a specific provider order. While checking the digoxin level is part of toxicity monitoring, the immediate safety action is to hold and notify.

distractorRationales.a: The clinical error in Administer the dose as scheduled since the order is valid is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, when the bedside priority is driven by A nurse is about to administer a scheduled dose of digoxin 0. Hold the dose and notify the provider of the patient's heart rate better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: Administer half the dose and recheck the heart rate in 30 minutes fits a lower-risk interpretation, rather than acting on Administering the full dose ignores a critical safety parameter. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay infection or sepsis escalation and leave infection progression unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: This option makes Check the patient's most recent serum digoxin level before acting the focus, when the bedside priority is driven by While checking the digoxin level is part of toxicity monitoring, the immediate safety action is to hold. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Hold the dose and notify the provider of the patient's heart rate targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

tags: Safety and Infection Control / Medication Safety
questions.takeaway: Digoxin safety rule: Hold and notify if apical HR < 60 bpm (adults). This applies regardless of whether the serum level is available. Safety parameters come before scheduled administration.

questions.stem: A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention. Which example correctly illustrates primary prevention?

options.text: Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40
Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season
Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture
Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps

questions.rationale: Primary prevention: actions taken before disease occurs to prevent onset. Examples: immunization, health education, smoking cessation counseling, fluoridated water, condom use. Secondary prevention: early detection of existing disease (screening) — mammography, colonoscopy, blood pressure screening, Pap smear. Tertiary prevention: reducing disability and restoring function in patients with established disease — rehabilitation, cardiac rehab, chronic disease management. Mammography and colonoscopy are secondary prevention (detecting disease before symptoms). Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention.

distractorRationales.a: This option overweights Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40, while the decisive cue is Not to be confused with clinical levels of care. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of circulation or perfusion and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture is that it anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, despite the clinical warning in Not to be confused with clinical levels of care. Choosing it can delay circulation and perfusion support and leave circulation or perfusion unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps is not the best answer, but the stem cue is Tertiary prevention: reducing disability and restoring function in patients with established disease — rehabilitation, cardiac rehab, chronic disease. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Primary prevention = before disease (vaccines, education); Secondary = early detection (screening); Tertiary = limit disability/restore function (rehab). Not to be confused with clinical levels of care.

questions.stem: A nurse has four patients to assess. Which patient should the nurse see FIRST, applying Maslow's hierarchy of needs?

options.text: A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure
A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%
A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home
A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10

questions.rationale: Maslow's hierarchy places physiological needs at the foundation. Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. A patient with RR 8 and SpO2 88% is in respiratory distress — an immediate life-threatening concern that takes absolute priority. Anxiety (A) is a safety/psychological need. Discharge teaching (C) is a self-actualization/cognitive need. Pain rated 4/10 (D) is physiological but not immediately life-threatening compared to respiratory failure.

distractorRationales.a: A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure fits a lower-risk interpretation, despite the clinical warning in Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: This option overweights A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home, although the nurse should be guided by Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The safer answer is A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88% because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: This option overweights A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10, although the nurse should be guided by Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Maslow hierarchy on NCLEX: airway/breathing/circulation (physiological) always come before safety, love/belonging, esteem, and self-actualization.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing an admission assessment on a 79-year-old patient transferred from a nursing facility. Which combination of factors identifies the HIGHEST fall risk?

options.text: Age >65 and female gender
History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg on standing)
Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29
Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion

questions.rationale: The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls in the elderly; (2) Polypharmacy with antihypertensives — alpha-blockers, diuretics, and calcium channel blockers commonly cause orthostatic hypotension and syncope; (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Both the Morse Fall Scale and STRATIFY risk tool weight prior falls most heavily. Key interventions: bed in lowest position, non-skid footwear, call light within reach, scheduled toileting (most falls occur during nighttime toileting), orthostatic vital signs before ambulation, and urgent medication review. Age and gender alone are poor predictors without functional factors.

distractorRationales.a: This answer choice treats Age >65 and female gender as the key issue, while the decisive cue is (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of medication toxicity and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: Selecting Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29 would be unsafe, rather than acting on (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The priority is History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic, not a step that delays stabilization of medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion, instead of responding to (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so The priority is History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic, not a step that delays stabilization of medication toxicity.

questions.takeaway: Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Prior fall is the strongest single predictor. Target modifiable risk factors aggressively.

questions.stem: An RN is caring for four patients and needs to delegate tasks. Which task is appropriate to assign to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP/CNA)?

options.text: Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea
Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube
Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery
Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care

questions.rationale: UAPs can perform tasks that are routine, repetitive, and do not require nursing judgment or assessment. Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the RN has assessed the patient's stability. Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Administering tube feedings (B) — in many states, enteral feeding via established G-tube can be delegated to trained UAPs, but the skill level required is greater than ambulation; this varies by state — ambulation is universally appropriate for CNA delegation. Patient education (D) is an RN responsibility requiring professional judgment.

distractorRationales.a: This choice focuses on Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea, rather than acting on Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.
distractorRationales.b: This distractor pulls attention toward Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube, even though the higher-risk finding is UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The problem with Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care is that it moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, despite the clinical warning in Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. The priority is Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching, and medication administration remain with the RN.

questions.stem: A patient delivers vaginally and 30 minutes later has an estimated blood loss of 750 mL with a soft, boggy uterus displaced to the right. BP is 94/58, HR 118. Which sequence of interventions is correct?

options.text: IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider
Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement
Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion
Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent

questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) from uterine atony management: (1) Massage the fundus — stimulates uterine contraction. (2) Empty the bladder — a full bladder displaces the uterus and prevents contraction (uterus displaced to the right = full bladder). (3) IV oxytocin — first-line uterotonic. (4) Notify provider if no response. Bladder emptying is critical and often overlooked — the displaced, boggy uterus signals bladder distension. Epinephrine (D) and hysterectomy are not indicated at this stage.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor pulls attention toward IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider, even though the higher-risk finding is Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) from uterine atony management: (1) Massage the fundus — stimulates uterine contraction. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: This option makes Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion the focus, but the stem cue is Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) from uterine atony management: (1) Massage the fundus — stimulates uterine contraction. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so The correct path is Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: This option overweights Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent, instead of responding to (4) Notify provider if no response. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so The priority is Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement, not a step that delays stabilization of bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: Boggy uterus displaced to the right after delivery = full bladder; empty bladder first, then fundal massage + oxytocin.

questions.stem: A post-operative patient on day 2 following abdominal surgery complains of left calf pain, warmth, and swelling. Vital signs are stable. Which action is the NURSE'S PRIORITY?

options.text: Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses
Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result
Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound
Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring

questions.rationale: Unilateral calf pain, warmth, and swelling in a post-operative patient are classic signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT). The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can be ordered promptly. Homan's sign (B) is unreliable and has been removed from clinical guidelines. Warmth and elevation (A) delay diagnosis. Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate.

distractorRationales.a: This answer choice treats Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses as the key issue, instead of responding to A post-operative patient on day 2 following abdominal surgery complains of left calf pain, warmth. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay circulation and perfusion support and leave circulation or perfusion unresolved.
distractorRationales.b: This distractor pulls attention toward Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result, even though the higher-risk finding is The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound is safer because it addresses circulation or perfusion before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring is not the best answer, and misses that Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound is safer because it addresses circulation or perfusion before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Post-op calf pain + swelling = suspect DVT; notify provider and anticipate duplex ultrasound — Homan's sign is unreliable.

questions.stem: A client with depression says: 'I don't see the point in trying anymore. Nothing I do matters.' Which nurse response is most therapeutic?

options.text: 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.'
'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'
'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.'
'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.'

questions.rationale: Option B is an open-ended exploration that invites the client to elaborate, demonstrating active listening without judgment. It also screens for suicidal ideation — 'nothing I do matters' and 'no point in trying' may signal passive suicidal thinking. Option A uses false reassurance ('so much to live for') and assumes understanding without exploring further. Option C minimizes the client's experience ('everyone feels that way'). Option D is non-therapeutic confrontation that dismisses the client's feelings.

distractorRationales.a: Selecting 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.' would be unsafe, rather than acting on therapeutic communication safety. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize 'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'; otherwise clinical safety risk remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on 'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.', rather than acting on therapeutic communication safety. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of clinical safety risk and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in 'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.' is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, even though the higher-risk finding is therapeutic communication safety. 'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.' better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Therapeutic communication: use open-ended statements to explore meaning, especially when statements may signal suicidal ideation.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with pneumonia who is receiving 4L/min via nasal cannula. The SpO2 is 91%. The nurse repositions the patient and administers the scheduled nebulizer treatment. Which finding would indicate the interventions were effective?

options.text: Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%
The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television
Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer
The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min

questions.rationale: Effective respiratory interventions produce measurable objective improvements in gas exchange: SpO2 rising to ≥ 94-95% and respiratory rate normalizing toward < 20/min indicate improved ventilation and oxygenation. Decreased anxiety may reflect improved oxygenation but is subjective and non-specific. Increased breath sounds after a nebulizer can indicate mobilization of secretions (which is initially normal), not necessarily a therapeutic response. Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions.

distractorRationales.b: The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television fits a lower-risk interpretation, while the decisive cue is Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer, but the stem cue is Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95% better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, even though the higher-risk finding is work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95% is safer because it addresses oxygenation or ventilation before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.
visualRationale.conclusion: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing vital signs in the pediatric unit. Which set of vital signs is within normal limits for a healthy 3-year-old toddler?
questions.rationale: Normal vital signs for a 3-year-old toddler: HR 80–130 bpm, RR 22–37 breaths/min, BP approximately 90–100/55–65 mmHg. HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 are all within expected ranges. Choice A has a bradycardic HR and adult-level BP. Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress, and RR 40 is elevated. Choice D has significant bradycardia. Key developmental milestone: vital signs normalize toward adult ranges with age — neonates have the highest HR and RR, lowest BP.

distractorRationales.a: HR 68, RR 14, BP 120/80 would not address the priority, and misses that Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward HR 145, RR 40, BP 80/48, without accounting for Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 is safer because it addresses oxygenation or ventilation before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: This option overweights HR 55, RR 18, BP 110/72, instead of responding to toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

visualRationale.caption: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.
visualRationale.conclusion: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.
